questions.question: What problem does GroupChat solve that static graphs handle poorly?
options: Graphs cannot contain more than 10 nodes
Real conversations aren't static — hardcoding every possible handoff causes edge explosion; GroupChat lets agents react to a shared pool with a selector picking the next speaker
Graphs cannot run on Windows
GroupChat removes the need for prompts
questions.explanation: Sometimes the coder asks the reviewer, sometimes the researcher. A shared pool that everyone reads plus a per-turn selector function is the archetype of emergent multi-agent conversation.

questions.question: What are the three selector flavors and their trade-offs?
options: Fast, medium, slow
Round-robin (deterministic but context-blind), LLM-selected (context-aware but adds an LLM call per turn), custom (e.g. LLM with fallback rules like 'verifier always follows coder')
Random, alphabetical, by seniority
Greedy, beam, sampling
questions.explanation: Round-robin gives the coder a turn during legal review; LLM selection reads the pool but costs a call every turn (AutoGen's default); custom functions blend both — deterministic guarantees where they matter, LLM judgment elsewhere.

questions.question: What are the three common termination patterns for a group chat?
options: Timeout, crash, restart
Max rounds (hard cap), a TERMINATE sentinel any agent can emit, and a goal-reached check by a lightweight verifier each turn
User closes the window
The selector resigns
questions.explanation: Without termination conditions a group chat is an unbounded loop with growing context. The verifier-driven stop is the strongest: the conversation ends when the goal is objectively met, not when patience runs out.

questions.question: What happened in the AutoGen → AG2 → Microsoft Agent Framework lineage?
options: AutoGen was deleted entirely

Microsoft rewrote AutoGen v0.4 as an event-driven actor model; the community forked v0.2's GroupChat semantics as AG2; in February 2026 AutoGen went to maintenance mode, merging with Semantic Kernel into Microsoft Agent Framework
AG2 is a Microsoft product; AutoGen is the fork
All three are unrelated projects
questions.explanation: The GroupChat primitive survives in both tracks — AG2 for v0.2-compatible code, Microsoft Agent Framework for the actor-model future. Learn the primitive once and the implementation churn stops mattering.

questions.question: In the GroupChat architecture, who actually holds the selector?
options: Each agent votes on the next speaker
The GroupChatManager — after each turn it invokes the selector, which returns the next agent, looping until a termination condition
The first agent registered
The user picks every speaker manually
questions.explanation: Agents just read the pool and speak when chosen; the manager owns turn-taking. This separation is what lets you swap round-robin for LLM selection or custom rules without touching the agents.
